title: Polynomial Bounds for the Grid-Minor Theorem
speaker: Julia Chuzhoy
abstract: One of the key results in Robertson and Seymour's seminal work on graph minors is the Grid-Minor Theorem (also known as the Excluded Grid Theorem). The theorem states that any graph of treewidth at least \(k\) contains a grid minor of size \(f(k)\) for some function \(f\). This theorem has found many applications in graph theory and algorithms.

proof_stages.label: Treewidth, well-linkedness, and quantitative grid minors
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up treewidth via tree decompositions, gives the equivalent well-linkedness viewpoint, defines grid minors, and frames the quantitative question of how large a grid minor treewidth k forces.

proof_stages.label: Path-of-sets system as the intermediate certificate
proof_stages.summary: A path-of-sets system is defined as ordered connected clusters joined by disjoint path bundles with well-linked interfaces, and the strategy is reduced to finding such a system of polynomial size in k.

proof_stages.label: Good clusters and aligned horizontal rows
proof_stages.summary: To extract a grid from a path-of-sets system, the proof treats the bundle paths as horizontal rows, uses direct-connection graphs inside clusters, applies the Leaf–Seymour tree-or-two-path dichotomy, and shows how good clusters can be aligned by intervening well-linked clusters.

proof_stages.label: Supercluster dichotomy completing path-of-sets to grid
proof_stages.summary: The simplifying assumption that every cluster is good is removed by grouping clusters into superclusters and proving that each supercluster either already contains a large grid minor or behaves as a good cluster.

proof_stages.label: Tree-of-sets systems from routing crossbars
proof_stages.summary: Routing work on well-linked edge- and node-disjoint paths supplies a crossbar-like structure, and after strengthening disjointness and allowing a degree-three tree organization it yields a tree-of-sets system in large-treewidth graphs.

proof_stages.label: Conversion from tree-of-sets to path-of-sets
proof_stages.summary: A tree-of-sets system is converted into a path-of-sets system either by taking a long root-to-leaf path or, in the low-height case, by sending many disjoint root-to-leaf paths and then stitching child path systems bottom-up while preserving enough surviving paths.

proof_stages.label: Polynomial grid-minor theorem and consequences
proof_stages.summary: Composing the large-treewidth-to-tree-of-sets construction, the tree-to-path conversion, and the path-to-grid extraction gives the first polynomial relation between treewidth and grid-minor size, with an efficient algorithm and a related congestion-two routing consequence.

examples.name: Benchmark treewidth values
examples.note: Trees, cycles, square grids, and constant-degree expanders are used to calibrate the definition of treewidth and the intuition that high treewidth means strong non-tree-like connectivity.

examples.name: Complete graph upper-bound obstruction
examples.note: The complete graph on n vertices illustrates why no grid-minor lower bound in terms of treewidth k can exceed order sqrt(k) in general.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the complete graph on n vertices to compute the treewidth obstruction and argues why a grid minor can have size at most about sqrt(n)

passes.reason: The complete graph is maximally symmetric and maximum-treewidth for its order, and is used to rule out grid-minor bounds above about sqrt(k).
The complete graph is maximally symmetric and has maximum treewidth on its vertex set, and it bounds any guaranteed grid minor side by vertex count.
The complete graph is maximally symmetric and maximal-treewidth, and is used to obstruct grid minors larger than about square-root size.
The complete graph is maximally symmetric and edge-dense and is used to obstruct any grid-minor bound larger than about sqrt(k).
The complete graph is maximally symmetric and maximum-treewidth/density, and it obstructs any grid-minor bound above order sqrt(k).

examples.name: Star in the direct-connection graph
examples.note: A star-shaped direct-connection graph shows why a cluster with many tree leaves is useful: after discarding the center horizontal path, the leaves can be connected through it without violating the chosen-row constraints.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the star-shaped direct-connection graph and explains that deleting the center path makes all remaining leaves directly connectable

passes.reason: The star is a highly symmetric extremal tree with maximum leaves and is posed as an apparent enemy case to the long-path strategy.
A star is a maximally leafy tree with no long direct path, used to show the naive long-path scenario can fail.
The star is an acyclic, leaf-extremal highly symmetric tree used as the bad no-long-path direct-connection case.
The star is an extremal hub-and-spoke tree with no long path, raised as an enemy case for the direct-connection graph.
A star is a highly leaf-symmetric tree maximizing the hub-and-leaf/high-leaf case, but it is exploited rather than used to refute the argument.
The star is a collapsed leaf-symmetric max-degree tree used as the enemy case to the hoped-for long direct path, though it becomes exploitable after discarding the hub.
The star is the collapsed hub-and-leaves case with maximal leaves and no long path, raised as the adversarial case for the naive long-path plan.
the star is an adversarial case for finding a long direct path and is a minimal tree with extremal hub-and-leaf form

examples.name: Two- and three-pair disjoint paths
examples.note: The contrast between the relatively clean k=2 disjoint-paths theorem and the much harder k=3 case illustrates the algorithmic routing context from which the crossbar machinery arose.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only describes known two- and three-pair disjoint-path results and their difficulty without solving or checking an instance

passes.reason: The two-pair case is the minimal nontrivial disjoint-paths setting, while three pairs mark the critical threshold where the simple analysis breaks.

examples.name: Failed DFS tour of a tree-of-sets system
examples.note: The natural attempt to traverse the degree-three tree by DFS exposes the obstruction that well-linkedness cannot support three independent routings through one cluster, motivating the later two-stage root-flow and stitching construction.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces the DFS-tour attempt on a tree-of-sets system and explains concretely why a degree-three cluster would require routing three path sets, which well-linkedness does not provide

passes.reason: The degree-three branching in the tree-of-sets system is the critical maximum-degree case that obstructs the naive DFS-tour construction.
The standard DFS tour idea is an obstruction to the construction because a degree-three cluster would require three simultaneous routings.
The DFS tour is an attempted construction that fails because a degree-three cluster would require three simultaneous routings.
The ordinary branching node in a tree-of-sets system is used to show why the naive DFS stitching tour fails.
The standard DFS-tour idea is shown to fail because a branching cluster would require three simultaneous routings beyond what well-linkedness supplies.
The degree-three DFS branching case is the subcubic boundary and shows the naive DFS stitching construction fails to route three sets from well-linkedness alone.
The DFS tour fails at the critical degree-three branch of a tree-of-sets system, where three simultaneous routings exceed what well-linkedness supplies.

examples.name: Large-girth expander obstruction
examples.note: In response to a question, a constant-degree expander with logarithmic girth is used to explain the stronger known upper obstruction of roughly sqrt(k/log k).
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker gives the large-girth expander obstruction argument, using expander treewidth and girth to bound how many grid cycles can fit

passes.reason: A constant-degree large-girth expander has extremal expansion and logarithmic girth and is used to obstruct grid minors larger than about sqrt(k/log k).
